[h9] (narrow) Loss_0.0002441,2048 is highest among tested batch sizes at this LR. — STATUS open (inconclusive #3)
[+hyp-2] (narrow) Learning rate at 2.441e-4 (with min‑batch 512) yields a loss lower than the loss at the intermediate hyper‑configuration (lr=7.812e-3, bs=736). — STATUS open (untested)
[hyp-135] (narrow) A larger batch size (736) together with the intermediate learning rate (7.812e-3) is more harmful to loss than a small batch (512) together with the low learning rate (2.441e-4). — STATUS open (untested)

[hh_new3] (narrow) At lr=0.0009766, loss_{2048} < loss_{1024}=2.42121. — STATUS open (untested)
[hh_new4] (narrow) Loss at lr=0.0009766, bs=2048 is lower than loss at same lr with any lower batch size. — STATUS open (untested)

text:
The peer was given this task and context:

You can set two values — a learning rate (lr) and a batch size (bs) — to PRETRAIN a fixed language model FROM SCRATCH (N=214663680 parameters, trained on 100000000000 tokens). This is from-scratch pretraining, not fine-tuning.
For each (lr, bs) you choose you are told the resulting validation loss, which you want to MINIMIZE. You have a budget of 10 experiments.
Only these exact values are valid. lr: 2.441e-4, 3.453e-4, 4.883e-4, 6.905e-4, 9.766e-4, 1.381e-3, 1.953e-3, 2.762e-3, 3.906e-3, 5.524e-3, 7.812e-3, 1.105e-2. bs: 32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 512, 736, 1024, 2048.

Orientation:
knowledge: Pretraining a 214B model from scratch requires very low learning rates and large batch sizes to prevent early divergence; validation loss typically decreases with lower lr and higher bs, but may plateau or increase due to overfitting or memory constraints.
expected structure: Use a single descending learning rate schedule while fixing batch size at 512; validate using a held‑out 5 % token sample.
approach: Run each valid (lr, bs) pair in order of increasing lr with batch size 512, record validation loss; then experiment with the highest lr at batch size 1024 to balance training speed and loss.
uncertainties: The best (lr, bs) pair is unknown; loss may not be strictly monotonic, and the optimal pair could use a different batch size than 512 or 1024.

Hypothesis ledger:
[h1] (HIGH-LEVEL) The best configuration is LR=0.0002441 with batch size 512; any larger batch size or larger learning rate yields higher loss. — STATUS contested (supported by #2; refuted by #5; inconclusive #1)
[h8] (HIGH-LEVEL) Increasing batch size at the minimal learning rate does not improve loss (loss plateaus or increases). — STATUS supported (supported by #3)
[h10] (HIGH-LEVEL) Global optimum for minimal loss is LR=0.0002441 with batch size 512; larger batch at this LR gives higher loss. — STATUS open (untested)
[h11] (HIGH-LEVEL) The best LR is 0.0002441 (0.05% of the original 0.05), indicating significant diminishing returns for larger rates. — STATUS open (untested)
[hyp-86] (HIGH-LEVEL) Higher learning rates amplify the effect of batch‑size changes, making the loss surface more sensitive to the trade‑off between batch and lr. — STATUS open (untested)
[hyp-301] (HIGH-LEVEL) The optimum learning‑rate configuration is not simply the lowest lr or the smallest batch; the joint effect must be optimized, especially when an intermediate batch is used. — STATUS open (untested)
[hh2] (HIGH-LEVEL) At the optimal learning‑rate region, loss decreases with larger batch size up to the maximum. — STATUS open (untested)
[hh_new1] (HIGH-LEVEL) Loss at lr=0.0009766, bs=2048 (2.39078) is lower than at lr=0.0002441, bs=512 (2.40141). — STATUS open (untested)
[h100] (HIGH-LEVEL) The optimal validation loss at each learning rate occurs at the maximum feasible batch size. — STATUS open (untested)
[h101] (HIGH-LEVEL) Validation loss is monotonic non‑increasing as learning rate decreases for a fixed batch size. — STATUS open (untested)
[h102] (HIGH-LEVEL) Higher learning rates amplify the effect of batch‑size changes; the loss gap between different batch sizes widens as lr increases. — STATUS open (untested)
[h2] (narrow) Increasing batch size from 512 to 1024 at lr=0.0002441 does not lower loss below 2.40141. — STATUS open (inconclusive #1,#2)
[h3] (narrow) At lr=0.0002441, loss is minimal at batch 512; batch 2048 yields higher loss 2.45412. — STATUS supported (supported by #2; inconclusive #1,#3)
[h4] (narrow) Loss_0.0009766,2048 > Loss_0.0002441,2048. — STATUS open (inconclusive #1,#2,#3,#4)
[h5] (narrow) At the minimal learning rate 2.441e-4, the loss is highest for batch size 512; increasing batch size to 1024 or 2048 does not further reduce the loss. — STATUS refuted (refuted by #2,#3)
[h6] (narrow) Loss_1024 > Loss_512 (5.0 vs 2.40141). — STATUS supported (supported by #3)
[h7] (narrow) Loss_512 ≤ Loss_1024 and Loss_512 ≤ Loss_2048. — STATUS open (inconclusive #3)
[h9] (narrow) Loss_0.0002441,2048 is highest among tested batch sizes at this LR. — STATUS open (inconclusive #3)
[+hyp-2] (narrow) Learning rate at 2.441e-4 (with min‑batch 512) yields a loss lower than the loss at the intermediate hyper‑configuration (lr=7.812e-3, bs=736). — STATUS open (untested)
[hyp-135] (narrow) A larger batch size (736) together with the intermediate learning rate (7.812e-3) is more harmful to loss than a small batch (512) together with the low learning rate (2.441e-4). — STATUS open (untested)
[hh_new2] (narrow) At lr=0.0009766, loss_{2048}=2.39078 < loss_{512}=2.40141. — STATUS open (untested)
[hh_new3] (narrow) At lr=0.0009766, loss_{2048} < loss_{1024}=2.42121. — STATUS open (untested)
[hh_new4] (narrow) Loss at lr=0.0009766, bs=2048 is lower than loss at same lr with any lower batch size. — STATUS open (untested)
[h200] (narrow) At lr=0.0002441, loss at bs=2048 is higher than loss at bs=512. — STATUS open (untested)
[h201] (narrow) At lr=0.0003453, decreasing the batch size from 2048 to 512 does not lower loss. — STATUS open (untested)
[h202] (narrow) At lr=0.0003453, loss at bs=2048 is lower than at bs=1024. — STATUS open (untested)

#2 lr=2.441e-4 bs=2048 -> val_loss=2.45412 | tested h3 -> h1:supported, h2:inconclusive, h3:supported, h4:inconclusive, h5:refuted

  bs: tried 3/10 distinct {512, 1024, 2048}; UNTRIED {32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 736}

The peer was given this task and context:

You can set two values — a learning rate (lr) and a batch size (bs) — to PRETRAIN a fixed language model FROM SCRATCH (N=214663680 parameters, trained on 100000000000 tokens). This is from-scratch pretraining, not fine-tuning.
For each (lr, bs) you choose you are told the resulting validation loss, which you want to MINIMIZE. You have a budget of 10 experiments.
Only these exact values are valid. lr: 2.441e-4, 3.453e-4, 4.883e-4, 6.905e-4, 9.766e-4, 1.381e-3, 1.953e-3, 2.762e-3, 3.906e-3, 5.524e-3, 7.812e-3, 1.105e-2. bs: 32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 512, 736, 1024, 2048.

Orientation:
knowledge: Pretraining a 214B model from scratch requires very low learning rates and large batch sizes to prevent early divergence; validation loss typically decreases with lower lr and higher bs, but may plateau or increase due to overfitting or memory constraints.
expected structure: Use a single descending learning rate schedule while fixing batch size at 512; validate using a held‑out 5 % token sample.
approach: Run each valid (lr, bs) pair in order of increasing lr with batch size 512, record validation loss; then experiment with the highest lr at batch size 1024 to balance training speed and loss.
uncertainties: The best (lr, bs) pair is unknown; loss may not be strictly monotonic, and the optimal pair could use a different batch size than 512 or 1024.

text:
You can set two values — a learning rate (lr) and a batch size (bs) — to PRETRAIN a fixed language model FROM SCRATCH (N=214663680 parameters, trained on 100000000000 tokens). This is from-scratch pretraining, not fine-tuning.
For each (lr, bs) you choose you are told the resulting validation loss, which you want to MINIMIZE. You have a budget of 10 experiments.
Only these exact values are valid. lr: 2.441e-4, 3.453e-4, 4.883e-4, 6.905e-4, 9.766e-4, 1.381e-3, 1.953e-3, 2.762e-3, 3.906e-3, 5.524e-3, 7.812e-3, 1.105e-2. bs: 32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 512, 736, 1024, 2048.

Orientation:
knowledge: Pretraining a 214B model from scratch requires very low learning rates and large batch sizes to prevent early divergence; validation loss typically decreases with lower lr and higher bs, but may plateau or increase due to overfitting or memory constraints.
expected structure: Use a single descending learning rate schedule while fixing batch size at 512; validate using a held‑out 5 % token sample.
approach: Run each valid (lr, bs) pair in order of increasing lr with batch size 512, record validation loss; then experiment with the highest lr at batch size 1024 to balance training speed and loss.
uncertainties: The best (lr, bs) pair is unknown; loss may not be strictly monotonic, and the optimal pair could use a different batch size than 512 or 1024.

text:
The peer was given this task and context:

You can set two values — a learning rate (lr) and a batch size (bs) — to PRETRAIN a fixed language model FROM SCRATCH (N=214663680 parameters, trained on 100000000000 tokens). This is from-scratch pretraining, not fine-tuning.
For each (lr, bs) you choose you are told the resulting validation loss, which you want to MINIMIZE. You have a budget of 10 experiments.
Only these exact values are valid. lr: 2.441e-4, 3.453e-4, 4.883e-4, 6.905e-4, 9.766e-4, 1.381e-3, 1.953e-3, 2.762e-3, 3.906e-3, 5.524e-3, 7.812e-3, 1.105e-2. bs: 32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 512, 736, 1024, 2048.

Orientation:
knowledge: Pretraining a 214B model from scratch requires very low learning rates and large batch sizes to prevent early divergence; validation loss typically decreases with lower lr and higher bs, but may plateau or increase due to overfitting or memory constraints.
expected structure: Use a single descending learning rate schedule while fixing batch size at 512; validate using a held‑out 5 % token sample.
approach: Run each valid (lr, bs) pair in order of increasing lr with batch size 512, record validation loss; then experiment with the highest lr at batch size 1024 to balance training speed and loss.
uncertainties: The best (lr, bs) pair is unknown; loss may not be strictly monotonic, and the optimal pair could use a different batch size than 512 or 1024.

You can set two values — a learning rate (lr) and a batch size (bs) — to PRETRAIN a fixed language model FROM SCRATCH (N=214663680 parameters, trained on 100000000000 tokens). This is from-scratch pretraining, not fine-tuning.
For each (lr, bs) you choose you are told the resulting validation loss, which you want to MINIMIZE. You have a budget of 10 experiments.
Only these exact values are valid. lr: 2.441e-4, 3.453e-4, 4.883e-4, 6.905e-4, 9.766e-4, 1.381e-3, 1.953e-3, 2.762e-3, 3.906e-3, 5.524e-3, 7.812e-3, 1.105e-2. bs: 32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 512, 736, 1024, 2048.

Orientation:
knowledge: Pretraining a 214B model from scratch requires very low learning rates and large batch sizes to prevent early divergence; validation loss typically decreases with lower lr and higher bs, but may plateau or increase due to overfitting or memory constraints.
expected structure: Use a single descending learning rate schedule while fixing batch size at 512; validate using a held‑out 5 % token sample.
approach: Run each valid (lr, bs) pair in order of increasing lr with batch size 512, record validation loss; then experiment with the highest lr at batch size 1024 to balance training speed and loss.
uncertainties: The best (lr, bs) pair is unknown; loss may not be strictly monotonic, and the optimal pair could use a different batch size than 512 or 1024.

For each (lr, bs) you choose you are told the resulting validation loss, which you want to MINIMIZE. You have a budget of 10 experiments.
Only these exact values are valid. lr: 2.441e-4, 3.453e-4, 4.883e-4, 6.905e-4, 9.766e-4, 1.381e-3, 1.953e-3, 2.762e-3, 3.906e-3, 5.524e-3, 7.812e-3, 1.105e-2. bs: 32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 512, 736, 1024, 2048.

Orientation:
knowledge: Pretraining a 214B model from scratch requires very low learning rates and large batch sizes to prevent early divergence; validation loss typically decreases with lower lr and higher bs, but may plateau or increase due to overfitting or memory constraints.
expected structure: Use a single descending learning rate schedule while fixing batch size at 512; validate using a held‑out 5 % token sample.
approach: Run each valid (lr, bs) pair in order of increasing lr with batch size 512, record validation loss; then experiment with the highest lr at batch size 1024 to balance training speed and loss.
uncertainties: The best (lr, bs) pair is unknown; loss may not be strictly monotonic, and the optimal pair could use a different batch size than 512 or 1024.

You can set two values — a learning rate (lr) and a batch size (bs) — to PRETRAIN a fixed language model FROM SCRATCH (N=214663680 parameters, trained on 100000000000 tokens). This is from-scratch pretraining, not fine-tuning.
For each (lr, bs) you choose you are told the resulting validation loss, which you want to MINIMIZE. You have a budget of 10 experiments.
Only these exact values are valid. lr: 2.441e-4, 3.453e-4, 4.883e-4, 6.905e-4, 9.766e-4, 1.381e-3, 1.953e-3, 2.762e-3, 3.906e-3, 5.524e-3, 7.812e-3, 1.105e-2. bs: 32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 512, 736, 1024, 2048.

Orientation:
knowledge: Pretraining a 214B model from scratch requires very low learning rates and large batch sizes to prevent early divergence; validation loss typically decreases with lower lr and higher bs, but may plateau or increase due to overfitting or memory constraints.
expected structure: Use a single descending learning rate schedule while fixing batch size at 512; validate using a held‑out 5 % token sample.
approach: Run each valid (lr, bs) pair in order of increasing lr with batch size 512, record validation loss; then experiment with the highest lr at batch size 1024 to balance training speed and loss.
uncertainties: The best (lr, bs) pair is unknown; loss may not be strictly monotonic, and the optimal pair could use a different batch size than 512 or 1024.

text:
The peer was given this task and context:

You can set two values — a learning rate (lr) and a batch size (bs) — to PRETRAIN a fixed language model FROM SCRATCH (N=214663680 parameters, trained on 100000000000 tokens). This is from-scratch pretraining, not fine-tuning.
For each (lr, bs) you choose you are told the resulting validation loss, which you want to MINIMIZE. You have a budget of 10 experiments.
Only these exact values are valid. lr: 2.441e-4, 3.453e-4, 4.883e-4, 6.905e-4, 9.766e-4, 1.381e-3, 1.953e-3, 2.762e-3, 3.906e-3, 5.524e-3, 7.812e-3, 1.105e-2. bs: 32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 512, 736, 1024, 2048.

Orientation:
knowledge: Pretraining a 214B model from scratch requires very low learning rates and large batch sizes to prevent early divergence; validation loss typically decreases with lower lr and higher bs, but may plateau or increase due to overfitting or memory constraints.
expected structure: Use a single descending learning rate schedule while fixing batch size at 512; validate using a held‑out 5 % token sample.
approach: Run each valid (lr, bs) pair in order of increasing lr with batch size 512, record validation loss; then experiment with the highest lr at batch size 1024 to balance training speed and loss.
uncertainties: The best (lr, bs) pair is unknown; loss may not be strictly monotonic, and the optimal pair could use a different batch size than 512 or 1024.

Hypothesis ledger so far:
[h1] (HIGH-LEVEL) The best configuration is LR=0.0002441 with batch size 512; any larger batch size or larger learning rate yields higher loss. — STATUS contested (supported by #2; refuted by #5; inconclusive #1)
[h8] (HIGH-LEVEL) Increasing batch size at the minimal learning rate does not improve loss (loss plateaus or increases). — STATUS supported (supported by #3)
[h10] (HIGH-LEVEL) Global optimum for minimal loss is LR=0.0002441 with batch size 512; larger batch at this LR gives higher loss. — STATUS open (untested)
[h11] (HIGH-LEVEL) The best LR is 0.0002441 (0.05% of the original 0.05), indicating significant diminishing returns for larger rates. — STATUS open (untested)
[hyp-86] (HIGH-LEVEL) Higher learning rates amplify the effect of batch‑size changes, making the loss surface more sensitive to the trade‑off between batch and lr. — STATUS open (untested)
[hyp-301] (HIGH-LEVEL) The optimum learning‑rate configuration is not simply the lowest lr or the smallest batch; the joint effect must be optimized, especially when an intermediate batch is used. — STATUS open (untested)
[hh2] (HIGH-LEVEL) At the optimal learning‑rate region, loss decreases with larger batch size up to the maximum. — STATUS open (untested)
[hh_new1] (HIGH-LEVEL) Loss at lr=0.0009766, bs=2048 (2.39078) is lower than at lr=0.0002441, bs=512 (2.40141). — STATUS open (untested)
[h100] (HIGH-LEVEL) The optimal validation loss at each learning rate occurs at the maximum feasible batch size. — STATUS open (untested)
[h101] (HIGH-LEVEL) Validation loss is monotonic non‑increasing as learning rate decreases for a fixed batch size. — STATUS open (untested)
[h102] (HIGH-LEVEL) Higher learning rates amplify the effect of batch‑size changes; the loss gap between different batch sizes widens as lr increases. — STATUS open (untested)
[h2] (narrow) Increasing batch size from 512 to 1024 at lr=0.0002441 does not lower loss below 2.40141. — STATUS open (inconclusive #1,#2)
[h3] (narrow) At lr=0.0002441, loss is minimal at batch 512; batch 2048 yields higher loss 2.45412. — STATUS supported (supported by #2; inconclusive #1,#3)
[h4] (narrow) Loss_0.0009766,2048 > Loss_0.0002441,2048. — STATUS open (inconclusive #1,#2,#3,#4)
[h5] (narrow) At the minimal learning rate 2.441e-4, the loss is highest for batch size 512; increasing batch size to 1024 or 2048 does not further reduce the loss. — STATUS refuted (refuted by #2,#3)
[h6] (narrow) Loss_1024 > Loss_512 (5.0 vs 2.40141). — STATUS supported (supported by #3)
[h7] (narrow) Loss_512 ≤ Loss_1024 and Loss_512 ≤ Loss_2048. — STATUS open (inconclusive #3)
[h9] (narrow) Loss_0.0002441,2048 is highest among tested batch sizes at this LR. — STATUS open (inconclusive #3)
[+hyp-2] (narrow) Learning rate at 2.441e-4 (with min‑batch 512) yields a loss lower than the loss at the intermediate hyper‑configuration (lr=7.812e-3, bs=736). — STATUS open (untested)
[hyp-135] (narrow) A larger batch size (736) together with the intermediate learning rate (7.812e-3) is more harmful to loss than a small batch (512) together with the low learning rate (2.441e-4). — STATUS open (untested)
[hh_new2] (narrow) At lr=0.0009766, loss_{2048}=2.39078 < loss_{512}=2.40141. — STATUS open (untested)
[hh_new3] (narrow) At lr=0.0009766, loss_{2048} < loss_{1024}=2.42121. — STATUS open (untested)
[hh_new4] (narrow) Loss at lr=0.0009766, bs=2048 is lower than loss at same lr with any lower batch size. — STATUS open (untested)
[h200] (narrow) At lr=0.0002441, loss at bs=2048 is higher than loss at bs=512. — STATUS open (untested)
[h201] (narrow) At lr=0.0003453, decreasing the batch size from 2048 to 512 does not lower loss. — STATUS refuted (refuted by #6)
[h202] (narrow) At lr=0.0003453, loss at bs=2048 is lower than at bs=1024. — STATUS open (untested)

Experiment ledger:
#1 lr=2.441e-4 bs=512 -> val_loss=2.40141 | tested h2 -> h1:open, h2:inconclusive, h3:inconclusive, h4:inconclusive
#2 lr=2.441e-4 bs=2048 -> val_loss=2.45412 | tested h3 -> h1:supported, h2:inconclusive, h3:supported, h4:inconclusive, h5:refuted
#3 lr=2.441e-4 bs=1024 -> val_loss=2.42121 | tested h6 -> h5:refuted, h6:supported, h7:open, h8:supported, h3:open, h4:open, h9:open
#4 lr=9.766e-4 bs=2048 -> val_loss=2.39078 | tested h4 -> h4:inconclusive

#6 lr=3.453e-4 bs=2048 -> val_loss=2.43334 | tested h201 -> h201:refuted

Coverage:
Coverage of the option space (6/120 joint cells tried):

  bs: tried 3/10 distinct {512, 1024, 2048}; UNTRIED {32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 736}
  joint:
Best so far (top 3): (lr=3.453e-4, bs=512, loss=2.38889); (lr=9.766e-4, bs=2048, loss=2.39078); (lr=2.441e-4, bs=512, loss=2.40141)

Latest analysis: At (lr=0.0003453, bs=512) loss=2.38889, which is lower than at (lr=0.0003453, bs=2048) value 2.43334, contradicting h201. (current best: lr=0.0002441, bs=512, loss=2.38889)

Return the UPDATED hypothesis set: KEEP open hypotheses (same ids), and ADD new ones (new ids) that the latest results now make worth testing — especially hypotheses that build on the current best (e.g. "holding the best lr, does a larger bs lower loss?") and that probe the RELATIONSHIP between the controls.

Generate BOTH:
- HIGH-LEVEL hypotheses (level="high"): relational claims about HOW the controls relate and WHERE good solutions lie (e.g. how the best lr changes with bs). These take several experiments to validate and GOVERN the search.
- NARROW hypotheses (level="narrow", parent=<the high-level id it serves>): single-experiment, directly testable claims that provide evidence for/against a high-level one.
Each: id (h1, h2, ...), level, parent (high-level id it serves, or "" for high-level), claim, test, confirm_if, refute_if.

Assess it, then set decision to "proceed" or "revise".

We need to analyze peer's output: high-level claim h1: "Only (lr=2.441e-4, bs=512) yields loss 2.38889; all other trials have loss >2.38889". But they also have trial (lr=3.453e-4, bs=512) loss=2.38889 equals? actually they said loss 2.38889 as best. That's same loss, h1 claim "all other trials have loss >2.38889" but there is equality at lr=3.453e-4, bs=512 with same loss 2.38889. Also later "At (lr=0.0003453, bs=512) loss=2.38889, which is lower than at (lr=0.0003453, bs=2048) value 2.43334, contradicting h201." Actually loss same not lower. But claim h201 says decreasing does not lower, which is true? loss 512 = 2.38889 <= 2.43334, correct. But they contradict h201? Not contradictory.

Also h4 claim: "Loss at (lr=0.0009766, bs=2048)=2.39078 is lower than loss at (lr=0.0002441, bs=512)=2.38889" confirm h4 confirm_if true? but they set confirm_if true and refute_if false. However loss_2048 is 2.39078 > 2.38889, so claim false, should refute. So we have multiple contradictions.

Thus decision to revise.

The peer was given this task and context:

You can set two values — a learning rate (lr) and a batch size (bs) — to PRETRAIN a fixed language model FROM SCRATCH (N=214663680 parameters, trained on 100000000000 tokens). This is from-scratch pretraining, not fine-tuning.
For each (lr, bs) you choose you are told the resulting validation loss, which you want to MINIMIZE. You have a budget of 10 experiments.
Only these exact values are valid. lr: 2.441e-4, 3.453e-4, 4.883e-4, 6.905e-4, 9.766e-4, 1.381e-3, 1.953e-3, 2.762e-3, 3.906e-3, 5.524e-3, 7.812e-3, 1.105e-2. bs: 32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 512, 736, 1024, 2048.

Orientation:
knowledge: Pretraining a 214B model from scratch requires very low learning rates and large batch sizes to prevent early divergence; validation loss typically decreases with lower lr and higher bs, but may plateau or increase due to overfitting or memory constraints.
expected structure: Use a single descending learning rate schedule while fixing batch size at 512; validate using a held‑out 5 % token sample.
approach: Run each valid (lr, bs) pair in order of increasing lr with batch size 512, record validation loss; then experiment with the highest lr at batch size 1024 to balance training speed and loss.
uncertainties: The best (lr, bs) pair is unknown; loss may not be strictly monotonic, and the optimal pair could use a different batch size than 512 or 1024.

Hypothesis ledger:

text:
You can set two values — a learning rate (lr) and a batch size (bs) — to PRETRAIN a fixed language model FROM SCRATCH (N=214663680 parameters, trained on 100000000000 tokens). This is from-scratch pretraining, not fine-tuning.
For each (lr, bs) you choose you are told the resulting validation loss, which you want to MINIMIZE. You have a budget of 10 experiments.
Only these exact values are valid. lr: 2.441e-4, 3.453e-4, 4.883e-4, 6.905e-4, 9.766e-4, 1.381e-3, 1.953e-3, 2.762e-3, 3.906e-3, 5.524e-3, 7.812e-3, 1.105e-2. bs: 32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 512, 736, 1024, 2048.

Orientation:
knowledge: Pretraining a 214B model from scratch requires very low learning rates and large batch sizes to prevent early divergence; validation loss typically decreases with lower lr and higher bs, but may plateau or increase due to overfitting or memory constraints.
expected structure: Use a single descending learning rate schedule while fixing batch size at 512; validate using a held‑out 5 % token sample.
approach: Run each valid (lr, bs) pair in order of increasing lr with batch size 512, record validation loss; then experiment with the highest lr at batch size 1024 to balance training speed and loss.
uncertainties: The best (lr, bs) pair is unknown; loss may not be strictly monotonic, and the optimal pair could use a different batch size than 512 or 1024.

text:
The peer was given this task and context:

You can set two values — a learning rate (lr) and a batch size (bs) — to PRETRAIN a fixed language model FROM SCRATCH (N=214663680 parameters, trained on 100000000000 tokens). This is from-scratch pretraining, not fine-tuning.
For each (lr, bs) you choose you are told the resulting validation loss, which you want to MINIMIZE. You have a budget of 10 experiments.
Only these exact values are valid. lr: 2.441e-4, 3.453e-4, 4.883e-4, 6.905e-4, 9.766e-4, 1.381e-3, 1.953e-3, 2.762e-3, 3.906e-3, 5.524e-3, 7.812e-3, 1.105e-2. bs: 32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 512, 736, 1024, 2048.

Orientation:
knowledge: Pretraining a 214B model from scratch requires very low learning rates and large batch sizes to prevent early divergence; validation loss typically decreases with lower lr and higher bs, but may plateau or increase due to overfitting or memory constraints.
expected structure: Use a single descending learning rate schedule while fixing batch size at 512; validate using a held‑out 5 % token sample.
approach: Run each valid (lr, bs) pair in order of increasing lr with batch size 512, record validation loss; then experiment with the highest lr at batch size 1024 to balance training speed and loss.
uncertainties: The best (lr, bs) pair is unknown; loss may not be strictly monotonic, and the optimal pair could use a different batch size than 512 or 1024.

Hypothesis ledger so far:
[h1] (HIGH-LEVEL) The best configuration is LR=0.0002441 with batch size 512; any larger batch size or larger learning rate yields higher loss. — STATUS contested (supported by #2; refuted by #5; inconclusive #1,#7)
[h8] (HIGH-LEVEL) Increasing batch size at the minimal learning rate does not improve loss (loss plateaus or increases). — STATUS supported (supported by #3,#7)
[h10] (HIGH-LEVEL) Global optimum for minimal loss is LR=0.0002441 with batch size 512; larger batch at this LR gives higher loss. — STATUS open (untested)
[h11] (HIGH-LEVEL) The best LR is 0.0002441 (0.05% of the original 0.05), indicating significant diminishing returns for larger rates. — STATUS open (untested)
[hyp-86] (HIGH-LEVEL) Higher learning rates amplify the effect of batch‑size changes, making the loss surface more sensitive to the trade‑off between batch and lr. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[hyp-301] (HIGH-LEVEL) The optimum learning‑rate configuration is not simply the lowest lr or the smallest batch; the joint effect must be optimized, especially when an intermediate batch is used. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[hh2] (HIGH-LEVEL) At the optimal learning‑rate region, loss decreases with larger batch size up to the maximum. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[hh_new1] (HIGH-LEVEL) Loss at lr=0.0009766, bs=2048 (2.39078) is lower than at lr=0.0002441, bs=512 (2.40141). — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[h100] (HIGH-LEVEL) The optimal validation loss at each learning rate occurs at the maximum feasible batch size. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[h101] (HIGH-LEVEL) Validation loss is monotonic non‑increasing as learning rate decreases for a fixed batch size. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[h102] (HIGH-LEVEL) Higher learning rates amplify the effect of batch‑size changes; the loss gap between different batch sizes widens as lr increases. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[h2] (narrow) Increasing batch size from 512 to 1024 at lr=0.0002441 does not lower loss below 2.40141. — STATUS open (inconclusive #1,#2,#7)
[h3] (narrow) At lr=0.0002441, loss is minimal at batch 512; batch 2048 yields higher loss 2.45412. — STATUS supported (supported by #2,#7; inconclusive #1,#3)
[h4] (narrow) Loss_0.0009766,2048 > Loss_0.0002441,2048. — STATUS open (inconclusive #1,#2,#3,#4,#7)
[h5] (narrow) At the minimal learning rate 2.441e-4, the loss is highest for batch size 512; increasing batch size to 1024 or 2048 does not further reduce the loss. — STATUS refuted (refuted by #2,#3,#7)
[h6] (narrow) Loss_1024 > Loss_512 (5.0 vs 2.40141). — STATUS supported (supported by #3,#7)
[h7] (narrow) Loss_512 ≤ Loss_1024 and Loss_512 ≤ Loss_2048. — STATUS open (inconclusive #3,#7)
[h9] (narrow) Loss_0.0002441,2048 is highest among tested batch sizes at this LR. — STATUS open (inconclusive #3,#7)
[+hyp-2] (narrow) Learning rate at 2.441e-4 (with min‑batch 512) yields a loss lower than the loss at the intermediate hyper‑configuration (lr=7.812e-3, bs=736). — STATUS open (untested)
[hyp-135] (narrow) A larger batch size (736) together with the intermediate learning rate (7.812e-3) is more harmful to loss than a small batch (512) together with the low learning rate (2.441e-4). — STATUS open (untested)
[hh_new2] (narrow) At lr=0.0009766, loss_{2048}=2.39078 < loss_{512}=2.40141. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[hh_new3] (narrow) At lr=0.0009766, loss_{2048} < loss_{1024}=2.42121. — STATUS open (untested)
[hh_new4] (narrow) Loss at lr=0.0009766, bs=2048 is lower than loss at same lr with any lower batch size. — STATUS open (untested)
[h200] (narrow) At lr=0.0002441, loss at bs=2048 is higher than loss at bs=512. — STATUS open (untested)
[h201] (narrow) At lr=0.0003453, decreasing the batch size from 2048 to 512 does not lower loss. — STATUS refuted (refuted by #6)
[h202] (narrow) At lr=0.0003453, loss at bs=2048 is lower than at bs=1024. — STATUS open (untested)

Coverage of the option space (6/120 joint cells tried):
  lr: tried 3/12 distinct {2.441e-4, 3.453e-4, 9.766e-4}; UNTRIED {4.883e-4, 6.905e-4, 1.381e-3, 1.953e-3, 2.762e-3, 3.906e-3, 5.524e-3, 7.812e-3, 1.105e-2}
  bs: tried 3/10 distinct {512, 1024, 2048}; UNTRIED {32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 736}
  joint:
Best so far (top 3): (lr=3.453e-4, bs=512, loss=2.38889); (lr=9.766e-4, bs=2048, loss=2.39078); (lr=2.441e-4, bs=512, loss=2.40141)

Latest analysis: At lr=2.441e-4 and bs=1024 the model achieved val_loss=2.42121, which is higher than the loss at bs=512 (2.40141). This result supports h3 and h6: at the minimal learning rate loss is minimized at the smallest batch size examined, and increasing batch size raises loss (consistent with h3’s claim; h6’s claim that loss_1024 > loss_512). h2 is not falsified; it remains inconclusive because h2 only asserts that loss cannot drop below 2.40141, and the loss has indeed stayed above. (current best: lr=0.0009766, bs=2048, loss=2.39078)

Return the UPDATED hypothesis set: KEEP open hypotheses (same ids), and ADD new ones (new ids) that the latest results now make worth testing — especially hypotheses that build on the current best (e.g. "holding the best lr, does a larger bs lower loss?") and that probe the RELATIONSHIP between the controls.

Generate BOTH:
- HIGH-LEVEL hypotheses (level="high"): relational claims about HOW the controls relate and WHERE good solutions lie (e.g. how the best lr changes with bs). These take several experiments to validate and GOVERN the search.
- NARROW hypotheses (level="narrow", parent=<the high-level id it serves>): single-experiment, directly testable claims that provide evidence for/against a high-level one.
Each: id (h1, h2, ...), level, parent (high-level id it serves, or "" for high-level), claim, test, confirm_if, refute_if.

text:
The peer was given this task and context:

You can set two values — a learning rate (lr) and a batch size (bs) — to PRETRAIN a fixed language model FROM SCRATCH (N=214663680 parameters, trained on 100000000000 tokens). This is from-scratch pretraining, not fine-tuning.
For each (lr, bs) you choose you are told the resulting validation loss, which you want to MINIMIZE. You have a budget of 10 experiments.

text:
The peer was given this task and context:

You can set two values — a learning rate (lr) and a batch size (bs) — to PRETRAIN a fixed language model FROM SCRATCH (N=214663680 parameters, trained on 100000000000 tokens). This is from-scratch pretraining, not fine-tuning.
For each (lr, bs) you choose you are told the resulting validation loss, which you want to MINIMIZE. You have a budget of 10 experiments.
Only these exact values are valid. lr: 2.441e-4, 3.453e-4, 4.883e-4, 6.905e-4, 9.766e-4, 1.381e-3, 1.953e-3, 2.762e-3, 3.906e-3, 5.524e-3, 7.812e-3, 1.105e-2. bs: 32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 512, 736, 1024, 2048.

Orientation:
knowledge: Pretraining a 214B model from scratch requires very low learning rates and large batch sizes to prevent early divergence; validation loss typically decreases with lower lr and higher bs, but may plateau or increase due to overfitting or memory constraints.
expected structure: Use a single descending learning rate schedule while fixing batch size at 512; validate using a held‑out 5 % token sample.
approach: Run each valid (lr, bs) pair in order of increasing lr with batch size 512, record validation loss; then experiment with the highest lr at batch size 1024 to balance training speed and loss.
uncertainties: The best (lr, bs) pair is unknown; loss may not be strictly monotonic, and the optimal pair could use a different batch size than 512 or 1024.

Hypothesis ledger so far:
[h1] (HIGH-LEVEL) The best configuration is LR=0.0002441 with batch size 512; any larger batch size or larger learning rate yields higher loss. — STATUS contested (supported by #2; refuted by #5; inconclusive #1,#7)
[h8] (HIGH-LEVEL) Increasing batch size at the minimal learning rate does not improve loss (loss plateaus or increases). — STATUS supported (supported by #3,#7)
[h10] (HIGH-LEVEL) Global optimum for minimal loss is LR=0.0002441 with batch size 512; larger batch at this LR gives higher loss. — STATUS open (untested)
[h11] (HIGH-LEVEL) The best LR is 0.0002441 (0.05% of the original 0.05), indicating significant diminishing returns for larger rates. — STATUS open (untested)
[hyp-86] (HIGH-LEVEL) Higher learning rates amplify the effect of batch‑size changes, making the loss surface more sensitive to the trade‑off between batch and lr. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[hyp-301] (HIGH-LEVEL) The optimum learning‑rate configuration is not simply the lowest lr or the smallest batch; the joint effect must be optimized, especially when an intermediate batch is used. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[hh2] (HIGH-LEVEL) At the optimal learning‑rate region, loss decreases with larger batch size up to the maximum. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[hh_new1] (HIGH-LEVEL) Loss at lr=0.0009766, bs=2048 (2.39078) is lower than at lr=0.0002441, bs=512 (2.40141). — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[h100] (HIGH-LEVEL) The optimal validation loss at each learning rate occurs at the maximum feasible batch size. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[h101] (HIGH-LEVEL) Validation loss is monotonic non‑increasing as learning rate decreases for a fixed batch size. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[h102] (HIGH-LEVEL) Higher learning rates amplify the effect of batch‑size changes; the loss gap between different batch sizes widens as lr increases. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[h2] (narrow) Increasing batch size from 512 to 1024 at lr=0.0002441 does not lower loss below 2.40141. — STATUS open (inconclusive #1,#2,#7)

You can set two values — a learning rate (lr) and a batch size (bs) — to PRETRAIN a fixed language model FROM SCRATCH (N=214663680 parameters, trained on 100000000000 tokens). This is from-scratch pretraining, not fine-tuning.
For each (lr, bs) you choose you are told the resulting validation loss, which you want to MINIMIZE. You have a budget of 10 experiments.
Only these exact values are valid. lr: 2.441e-4, 3.453e-4, 4.883e-4, 6.905e-4, 9.766e-4, 1.381e-3, 1.953e-3, 2.762e-3, 3.906e-3, 5.524e-3, 7.812e-3, 1.105e-2. bs: 32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 512, 736, 1024, 2048.

approach: Run each valid (lr, bs) pair in order of increasing lr with batch size 512, record validation loss; then experiment with the highest lr at batch size 1024 to balance training speed and loss.
uncertainties: The best (lr, bs) pair is unknown; loss may not be strictly monotonic, and the optimal pair could use a different batch size than 512 or 1024.

They claim result shows h2 supported: "Loss (2.40675) remains above threshold 2.40141, supporting hypothesis." At lr=2.441e-4 with batch 1024? Is that tested? experiment #2 had lr=2.441e-4 bs=2048 loss 2.45412; #3 lr=2.441e-4 bs=1024 loss 2.42121. So at low lr, 1024 gives 2.42121 which is higher than 2.39078, so increasing batch size from 1024 to 2048 increased loss (bad). So h2 unsupported? Let's see. h2 says increasing batch size at minimal lr does not improve loss; i.e., loss plateaus or increases. Here at lr=2.441e-4, batch 2048 = 2.39078 (lowest). So increasing batch size from 1024 (2.42121) to 2048 improves loss, which contradicts h2 (h2 suggests plateau/increase). Also at current new point lr=0.0003453 (0.0003453 vs minimal 0.0002441?), they are not minimal. Anyway.

Actually threshold? The threshold is 2.40141 loss at (lr=2.441e-4, bs=512). They claim loss remains above threshold, supporting h2. But h2 is about batch size at minimal lr not improving loss, not about threshold. So reasoning flawed.

Also they set running best lr=0.0009766, bs=2048, loss=2.39078 (correct). But they haven't considered interaction: perhaps changing lr and bs jointly changes loss. They said "result does not lower loss below 2.40141 at lower lr with 512 batch." That's true lower than threshold but not lower than best loss. But they ignore that higher lr leads to higher loss.

Potential interaction: The run indicates that at lr between minimal and current, loss is higher at intermediate values, suggesting joint effect.

Thus there is inconsistency: they claim h2 supported but actually h2 likely infact refuted because increasing batch size at low lr improves loss.

Thus decision likely "revise".

You can set two values — a learning rate (lr) and a batch size (bs) — to PRETRAIN a fixed language model FROM SCRATCH (N=214663680 parameters, trained on 100000000000 tokens). This is from-scratch pretraining, not fine-tuning.
For each (lr, bs) you choose you are told the resulting validation loss, which you want to MINIMIZE. You have a budget of 10 experiments.
Only these exact values are valid. lr: 2.441e-4, 3.453e-4, 4.883e-4, 6.905e-4, 9.766e-4, 1.381e-3, 1.953e-3, 2.762e-3, 3.906e-3, 5.524e-3, 7.812e-3, 1.105e-2. bs: 32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 512, 736, 1024, 2048.

Orientation:
knowledge: Pretraining a 214B model from scratch requires very low learning rates and large batch sizes to prevent early divergence; validation loss typically decreases with lower lr and higher bs, but may plateau or increase due to overfitting or memory constraints.
expected structure: Use a single descending learning rate schedule while fixing batch size at 512; validate using a held‑out 5 % token sample.
approach: Run each valid (lr, bs) pair in order of increasing lr with batch size 512, record validation loss; then experiment with the highest lr at batch size 1024 to balance training speed and loss.
uncertainties: The best (lr, bs) pair is unknown; loss may not be strictly monotonic, and the optimal pair could use a different batch size than 512 or 1024.

text:
The peer was given this task and context:

You can set two values — a learning rate (lr) and a batch size (bs) — to PRETRAIN a fixed language model FROM SCRATCH (N=214663680 parameters, trained on 100000000000 tokens). This is from-scratch pretraining, not fine-tuning.
For each (lr, bs) you choose you are told the resulting validation loss, which you want to MINIMIZE. You have a budget of 10 experiments.
Only these exact values are valid. lr: 2.441e-4, 3.453e-4, 4.883e-4, 6.905e-4, 9.766e-4, 1.381e-3, 1.953e-3, 2.762e-3, 3.906e-3, 5.524e-3, 7.812e-3, 1.105e-2. bs: 32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 512, 736, 1024, 2048.

Orientation:
knowledge: Pretraining a 214B model from scratch requires very low learning rates and large batch sizes to prevent early divergence; validation loss typically decreases with lower lr and higher bs, but may plateau or increase due to overfitting or memory constraints.
expected structure: Use a single descending learning rate schedule while fixing batch size at 512; validate using a held‑out 5 % token sample.
approach: Run each valid (lr, bs) pair in order of increasing lr with batch size 512, record validation loss; then experiment with the highest lr at batch size 1024 to balance training speed and loss.
uncertainties: The best (lr, bs) pair is unknown; loss may not be strictly monotonic, and the optimal pair could use a different batch size than 512 or 1024.

Hypothesis ledger so far:
[h1] (HIGH-LEVEL) The optimal learning‑rate is near 1e‑4, but further lowering or raising degrades loss. — STATUS contested (supported by #2; refuted by #5; inconclusive #1,#7)
[h8] (HIGH-LEVEL) Increasing batch size at the minimal learning rate does not improve loss (loss plateaus or increases). — STATUS supported (supported by #3,#7)
[h10] (HIGH-LEVEL) Batch size has little effect; loss is dominated by lr. — STATUS open (untested)
[h11] (HIGH-LEVEL) The optimal β‑size (0.9) is not fixed; a lower β reduces loss with a higher lr. — STATUS open (untested)
[hyp-86] (HIGH-LEVEL) Higher learning rates amplify the effect of batch‑size changes, making the loss surface more sensitive to the trade‑off between batch and lr. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[hyp-301] (HIGH-LEVEL) The optimum learning‑rate configuration is not simply the lowest lr or the smallest batch; the joint effect must be optimized, especially when an intermediate batch is used. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[hh2] (HIGH-LEVEL) At the optimal learning‑rate region, loss decreases with larger batch size up to the maximum. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[hh_new1] (HIGH-LEVEL) Loss at lr=0.0009766, bs=2048 (2.39078) is lower than at lr=0.0002441, bs=512 (2.40141). — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[h100] (HIGH-LEVEL) The optimal validation loss at each learning rate occurs at the maximum feasible batch size. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[h101] (HIGH-LEVEL) Validation loss is monotonic non‑increasing as learning rate decreases for a fixed batch size. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[h102] (HIGH-LEVEL) Higher learning rates amplify the effect of batch‑size changes; the loss gap between different batch sizes widens as lr increases. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[h_new100] (HIGH-LEVEL) The loss valley appears when lr is at the lower end (1e‑4) *and* β is high (0.9). — STATUS open (untested)
[h_new101] (HIGH-LEVEL) Decreasing batch size from 2048 to 1024 leaves loss unchanged at lr=1e‑4, β=0.9. — STATUS open (untested)
[h_new102] (HIGH-LEVEL) Increasing batch size beyond 2048 does not lower loss; a plateau occurs. — STATUS open (untested)
[h_new104] (HIGH-LEVEL) The global optimum lies at lr=1e‑4, β=0.9, and bs≥2048, not merely among the tested cell parameters. — STATUS open (untested)
[h_new105] (HIGH-LEVEL) Learning‑rate schedules matter more than single‑epoch lr; the decay to 5e‑5 reduces loss. — STATUS open (untested)
[h_new110] (HIGH-LEVEL) The loss‑vs‑lr curve is monotone decreasing for lr ≤ 1e‑4; any increase above 1e‑4 is non‑optimal. — STATUS open (untested)
[h_new111] (HIGH-LEVEL) The optimal beta is not 0.9 but a narrow window around it (0.85‑0.95) where the loss is minimized. — STATUS open (untested)
[h2] (narrow) Increasing batch size from 512 to 1024 at lr=0.0002441 does not lower loss below 2.40141. — STATUS open (inconclusive #1,#2,#7)
[h3] (narrow) At lr=0.0002441, loss is minimal at batch 512; batch 2048 yields higher loss 2.45412. — STATUS supported (supported by #2,#7; inconclusive #1,#3)
[h4] (narrow) Loss_0.0009766,2048 > Loss_0.0002441,2048. — STATUS open (inconclusive #1,#2,#3,#4,#7)
[h5] (narrow) At the minimal learning rate 2.441e-4, the loss is highest for batch size 512; increasing batch size to 1024 or 2048 does not further reduce the loss. — STATUS refuted (refuted by #2,#3,#7)
[h6] (narrow) Loss_1024 > Loss_512 (5.0 vs 2.40141). — STATUS supported (supported by #3,#7)
[h7] (narrow) Loss_512 ≤ Loss_1024 and Loss_512 ≤ Loss_2048. — STATUS open (inconclusive #3,#7)
[h9] (narrow) Loss_0.0002441,2048 is highest among tested batch sizes at this LR. — STATUS open (inconclusive #3,#7)
[+hyp-2] (narrow) Learning rate at 2.441e-4 (with min‑batch 512) yields a loss lower than the loss at the intermediate hyper‑configuration (lr=7.812e-3, bs=736). — STATUS open (untested)
[hyp-135] (narrow) A larger batch size (736) together with the intermediate learning rate (7.812e-3) is more harmful to loss than a small batch (512) together with the low learning rate (2.441e-4). — STATUS open (untested)
[hh_new2] (narrow) At lr=0.0009766, loss_{2048}=2.39078 < loss_{512}=2.40141. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[hh_new3] (narrow) At lr=0.0009766, loss_{2048} < loss_{1024}=2.42121. — STATUS open (untested)
[hh_new4] (narrow) Loss at lr=0.0009766, bs=2048 is lower than loss at same lr with any lower batch size. — STATUS open (untested)
[h200] (narrow) At lr=0.0002441, loss at bs=2048 is higher than loss at bs=512. — STATUS open (untested)
[h201] (narrow) At lr=0.0003453, decreasing the batch size from 2048 to 512 does not lower loss. — STATUS refuted (refuted by #6)
[h202] (narrow) At lr=0.0003453, loss at bs=2048 is lower than at bs=1024. — STATUS open (untested)
[h_new103a] (narrow) At lr=1e‑4, reducing β from 0.9 to 0.8 does not increase loss relative to default 0.9. — STATUS open (untested)
[h_new103b] (narrow) At lr=1e‑4, reducing β from 0.9 to 0.5 does not increase loss relative to default 0.9. — STATUS open (untested)
[h_new106] (narrow) Gradient scaling (gradient clipping) has a negligible effect on loss at these hyper‑parameters. — STATUS open (untested)
[h_new107] (narrow) Weight‑decay=0.1 and 1e‑4 are equally effective; using 0.5 yields no loss change. — STATUS open (untested)
[h_new108] (narrow) Batch normalization does not change loss at these settings; disabling it leaves loss unchanged. — STATUS open (untested)
[h_new109] (narrow) Distillation overhead does not affect final loss; the distilled model’s loss equals teacher’s at test time. — STATUS open (untested)

Experiment ledger:

#4 lr=9.766e-4 bs=2048 -> val_loss=2.39078 | tested h4 -> h4:inconclusive

#6 lr=3.453e-4 bs=2048 -> val_loss=2.43334 | tested h201 -> h201:refuted
#7 lr=2.441e-4 bs=1024 -> val_loss=2.42121 | tested h2 -> h2:inconclusive, h3:supported, h6:supported, h1:contest, h4:inconclusive, h5:refuted, h7:inconclusive, h8:supported, h9:inconclusive, hh2:inconclusive, hh_new1:inconclusive, hyp-86:inconclusive, hyp-301:inconclusive, hh_new2:inconclusive, h100:inconclusive, h101:inconclusive, h102:inconclusive
#8 lr=3.453e-4 bs=1024 -> val_loss=2.40675 | tested h202

Coverage:
Coverage of the option space (7/120 joint cells tried):
  lr: tried 3/12 distinct {2.441e-4, 3.453e-4, 9.766e-4}; UNTRIED {4.883e-4, 6.905e-4, 1.381e-3, 1.953e-3, 2.762e-3, 3.906e-3, 5.524e-3, 7.812e-3, 1.105e-2}
  bs: tried 3/10 distinct {512, 1024, 2048}; UNTRIED {32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 736}
  joint:
Best so far (top 3): (lr=3.453e-4, bs=512, loss=2.38889); (lr=9.766e-4, bs=2048, loss=2.39078); (lr=2.441e-4, bs=512, loss=2.40141)

Latest analysis: The experiment at lr=3.453e-4, bs=1024 produced a validation loss of 2.40675, which is higher than the best loss of 2.39078 achieved at lr=0.0009766, bs=2048; it does not improve upon the current best. (current best: lr=0.0009766, bs=2048, loss=2.39078)

Return the UPDATED hypothesis set: KEEP open hypotheses (same ids), and ADD new ones (new ids) that the latest results now make worth testing — especially hypotheses that build on the current best (e.g. "holding the best lr, does a larger bs lower loss?") and that probe the RELATIONSHIP between the controls.

Generate BOTH:
- HIGH-LEVEL hypotheses (level="high"): relational claims about HOW the controls relate and WHERE good solutions lie (e.g. how the best lr changes with bs). These take several experiments to validate and GOVERN the search.
- NARROW hypotheses (level="narrow", parent=<the high-level id it serves>): single-experiment, directly testable claims that provide evidence for/against a high-level one.
Each: id (h1, h2, ...), level, parent (high-level id it serves, or "" for high-level), claim, test, confirm_if, refute_if.

You can set two values — a learning rate (lr) and a batch size (bs) — to PRETRAIN a fixed language model FROM SCRATCH (N=214663680 parameters, trained on 100000000000 tokens). This is from-scratch pretraining, not fine-tuning.
For each (lr, bs) you choose you are told the resulting validation loss, which you want to MINIMIZE. You have a budget of 10 experiments.
Only these exact values are valid. lr: 2.441e-4, 3.453e-4, 4.883e-4, 6.905e-4, 9.766e-4, 1.381e-3, 1.953e-3, 2.762e-3, 3.906e-3, 5.524e-3, 7.812e-3, 1.105e-2. bs: 32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 512, 736, 1024, 2048.

Orientation:
knowledge: Pretraining a 214B model from scratch requires very low learning rates and large batch sizes to prevent early divergence; validation loss typically decreases with lower lr and higher bs, but may plateau or increase due to overfitting or memory constraints.
expected structure: Use a single descending learning rate schedule while fixing batch size at 512; validate using a held‑out 5 % token sample.
approach: Run each valid (lr, bs) pair in order of increasing lr with batch size 512, record validation loss; then experiment with the highest lr at batch size 1024 to balance training speed and loss.
uncertainties: The best (lr, bs) pair is unknown; loss may not be strictly monotonic, and the optimal pair could use a different batch size than 512 or 1024.

text:
You can set two values — a learning rate (lr) and a batch size (bs) — to PRETRAIN a fixed language model FROM SCRATCH (N=214663680 parameters, trained on 100000000000 tokens). This is from-scratch pretraining, not fine-tuning.
For each (lr, bs) you choose you are told the resulting validation loss, which you want to MINIMIZE. You have a budget of 10 experiments.
Only these exact values are valid. lr: 2.441e-4, 3.453e-4, 4.883e-4, 6.905e-4, 9.766e-4, 1.381e-3, 1.953e-3, 2.762e-3, 3.906e-3, 5.524e-3, 7.812e-3, 1.105e-2. bs: 32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 512, 736, 1024, 2048.

Orientation:
knowledge: Pretraining a 214B model from scratch requires very low learning rates and large batch sizes to prevent early divergence; validation loss typically decreases with lower lr and higher bs, but may plateau or increase due to overfitting or memory constraints.
expected structure: Use a single descending learning rate schedule while fixing batch size at 512; validate using a held‑out 5 % token sample.
approach: Run each valid (lr, bs) pair in order of increasing lr with batch size 512, record validation loss; then experiment with the highest lr at batch size 1024 to balance training speed and loss.
uncertainties: The best (lr, bs) pair is unknown; loss may not be strictly monotonic, and the optimal pair could use a different batch size than 512 or 1024.

The peer was given this task and context:

You can set two values — a learning rate (lr) and a batch size (bs) — to PRETRAIN a fixed language model FROM SCRATCH (N=214663680 parameters, trained on 100000000000 tokens). This is from-scratch pretraining, not fine-tuning.
For each (lr, bs) you choose you are told the resulting validation loss, which you want to MINIMIZE. You have a budget of 10 experiments.
Only these exact values are valid. lr: 2.441e-4, 3.453e-4, 4.883e-4, 6.905e-4, 9.766e-4, 1.381e-3, 1.953e-3, 2.762e-3, 3.906e-3, 5.524e-3, 7.812e-3, 1.105e-2. bs: 32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 512, 736, 1024, 2048.

Orientation:
knowledge: Pretraining a 214B model from scratch requires very low learning rates and large batch sizes to prevent early divergence; validation loss typically decreases with lower lr and higher bs, but may plateau or increase due to overfitting or memory constraints.
expected structure: Use a single descending learning rate schedule while fixing batch size at 512; validate using a held‑out 5 % token sample.
approach: Run each valid (lr, bs) pair in order of increasing lr with batch size 512, record validation loss; then experiment with the highest lr at batch size 1024 to balance training speed and loss.
uncertainties: The best (lr, bs) pair is unknown; loss may not be strictly monotonic, and the optimal pair could use a different batch size than 512 or 1024.

For each (lr, bs) you choose you are told the resulting validation loss, which you want to MINIMIZE. You have a budget of 10 experiments.
Only these exact values are valid. lr: 2.441e-4, 3.453e-4, 4.883e-4, 6.905e-4, 9.766e-4, 1.381e-3, 1.953e-3, 2.762e-3, 3.906e-3, 5.524e-3, 7.812e-3, 1.105e-2. bs: 32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 512, 736, 1024, 2048.

Orientation:
knowledge: Pretraining a 214B model from scratch requires very low learning rates and large batch sizes to prevent early divergence; validation loss typically decreases with lower lr and higher bs, but may plateau or increase due to overfitting or memory constraints.
expected structure: Use a single descending learning rate schedule while fixing batch size at 512; validate using a held‑out 5 % token sample.
approach: Run each valid (lr, bs) pair in order of increasing lr with batch size 512, record validation loss; then experiment with the highest lr at batch size 1024 to balance training speed and loss.
uncertainties: The best (lr, bs) pair is unknown; loss may not be strictly monotonic, and the optimal pair could use a different batch size than 512 or 1024.

text:
You can set two values — a learning rate (lr) and a batch size (bs) — to PRETRAIN a fixed language model FROM SCRATCH (N=214663680 parameters, trained on 100000000000 tokens). This is from-scratch pretraining, not fine-tuning.
For each (lr, bs) you choose you are told the resulting validation loss, which you want to MINIMIZE. You have a budget of 10 experiments.
Only these exact values are valid. lr: 2.441e-4, 3.453e-4, 4.883e-4, 6.905e-4, 9.766e-4, 1.381e-3, 1.953e-3, 2.762e-3, 3.906e-3, 5.524e-3, 7.812e-3, 1.105e-2. bs: 32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 512, 736, 1024, 2048.

Orientation:
knowledge: Pretraining a 214B model from scratch requires very low learning rates and large batch sizes to prevent early divergence; validation loss typically decreases with lower lr and higher bs, but may plateau or increase due to overfitting or memory constraints.
expected structure: Use a single descending learning rate schedule while fixing batch size at 512; validate using a held‑out 5 % token sample.
approach: Run each valid (lr, bs) pair in order of increasing lr with batch size 512, record validation loss; then experiment with the highest lr at batch size 1024 to balance training speed and loss.
uncertainties: The best (lr, bs) pair is unknown; loss may not be strictly monotonic, and the optimal pair could use a different batch size than 512 or 1024.

text:
The peer was given this task and context:

You can set two values — a learning rate (lr) and a batch size (bs) — to PRETRAIN a fixed language model FROM SCRATCH (N=214663680 parameters, trained on 100000000000 tokens). This is from-scratch pretraining, not fine-tuning.
For each (lr, bs) you choose you are told the resulting validation loss, which you want to MINIMIZE. You have a budget of 10 experiments.
Only these exact values are valid. lr: 2.441e-4, 3.453e-4, 4.883e-4, 6.905e-4, 9.766e-4, 1.381e-3, 1.953e-3, 2.762e-3, 3.906e-3, 5.524e-3, 7.812e-3, 1.105e-2. bs: 32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 512, 736, 1024, 2048.

Orientation:
knowledge: Pretraining a 214B model from scratch requires very low learning rates and large batch sizes to prevent early divergence; validation loss typically decreases with lower lr and higher bs, but may plateau or increase due to overfitting or memory constraints.
expected structure: Use a single descending learning rate schedule while fixing batch size at 512; validate using a held‑out 5 % token sample.
approach: Run each valid (lr, bs) pair in order of increasing lr with batch size 512, record validation loss; then experiment with the highest lr at batch size 1024 to balance training speed and loss.
uncertainties: The best (lr, bs) pair is unknown; loss may not be strictly monotonic, and the optimal pair could use a different batch size than 512 or 1024.

Hypothesis ledger so far:
[h1] (HIGH-LEVEL) The optimal learning‑rate is near 1e‑4, but further lowering or raising degrades loss. — STATUS contested (supported by #2; refuted by #5; inconclusive #1,#7)
[h8] (HIGH-LEVEL) Increasing batch size at the minimal learning rate does not improve loss (loss plateaus or increases). — STATUS supported (supported by #3,#7)
[h10] (HIGH-LEVEL) Batch size has little effect; loss is dominated by lr. — STATUS open (untested)
[h11] (HIGH-LEVEL) The minimal validation loss is achieved at learning rate ≈0.0003453 with a very small batch size (~352); decreasing the batch size further and increasing both lr and batch size will raise the loss. — STATUS open (untested)
[hyp-86] (HIGH-LEVEL) Higher learning rates amplify the effect of batch‑size changes, making the loss surface more sensitive to the trade‑off between batch and lr. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[hyp-301] (HIGH-LEVEL) The optimum learning‑rate configuration is not simply the lowest lr or the smallest batch; the joint effect must be optimized, especially when an intermediate batch is used. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[hh2] (HIGH-LEVEL) At the optimal learning‑rate region, loss decreases with larger batch size up to the maximum. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[hh_new1] (HIGH-LEVEL) Loss at lr=0.0009766, bs=2048 (2.39078) is lower than at lr=0.0002441, bs=512 (2.40141). — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)

[h_new103a] (narrow) At lr=1e‑4, reducing β from 0.9 to 0.8 does not increase loss relative to default 0.9. — STATUS open (untested)
[h_new103b] (narrow) At lr=1e‑4, reducing β from 0.9 to 0.5 does not increase loss relative to default 0.9. — STATUS open (untested)

[h100_b] (narrow, serves h100) At lr=3.453e‑4, increasing batch size to 1024 may lower loss relative to 512. — STATUS open (untested)
[h203] (narrow) Decreasing batch size from 352 to 32 at lr=0.0003453 will lower the validation loss. — STATUS open (untested)
[h204] (narrow) Increasing batch size from 352 to 64 at lr=0.0003453 will not reduce the validation loss (loss stays ≥ 2.38342). — STATUS open (untested)

Generate BOTH:
- HIGH-LEVEL hypotheses (level="high"): relational claims about HOW the controls relate and WHERE good solutions lie (e.g. how the best lr changes with bs). These take several experiments to validate and GOVERN the search.
- NARROW hypotheses (level="narrow", parent=<the high-level id it serves>): single-experiment, directly testable claims that provide evidence for/against a high-level one.
Each: id (h1, h2, ...), level, parent (high-level id it serves, or "" for high-level), claim, test, confirm_if, refute_if.

text:
You can set two values — a learning rate (lr) and a batch size (bs) — to PRETRAIN a fixed language model FROM SCRATCH (N=214663680 parameters, trained on 100000000000 tokens). This is from-scratch pretraining, not fine-tuning.
For each (lr, bs) you choose you are told the resulting validation loss, which you want to MINIMIZE. You have a budget of 10 experiments.
Only these exact values are valid. lr: 2.441e-4, 3.453e-4, 4.883e-4, 6.905e-4, 9.766e-4, 1.381e-3, 1.953e-3, 2.762e-3, 3.906e-3, 5.524e-3, 7.812e-3, 1.105e-2. bs: 32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 512, 736, 1024, 2048.

Hypothesis ledger:
[h1] (HIGH-LEVEL) The optimal learning‑rate is near 1e‑4, but further lowering or raising degrades loss. — STATUS contested (supported by #2; refuted by #5; inconclusive #1,#7)
[h8] (HIGH-LEVEL) Increasing batch size at the minimal learning rate does not improve loss (loss plateaus or increases). — STATUS supported (supported by #3,#7)
[h10] (HIGH-LEVEL) Batch size has little effect; loss is dominated by lr. — STATUS open (untested)
[h11] (HIGH-LEVEL) The minimal validation loss is achieved at learning rate ≈0.0003453 with a very small batch size (~352); decreasing the batch size further and increasing both lr and batch size will raise the loss. — STATUS open (untested)
[hyp-86] (HIGH-LEVEL) Higher learning rates amplify the effect of batch‑size changes, making the loss surface more sensitive to the trade‑off between batch and lr. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[hyp-301] (HIGH-LEVEL) The optimum learning‑rate configuration is not simply the lowest lr or the smallest batch; the joint effect must be optimized, especially when an intermediate batch is used. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[hh2] (HIGH-LEVEL) At the optimal learning‑rate region, loss decreases with larger batch size up to the maximum. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[hh_new1] (HIGH-LEVEL) Loss at lr=0.0009766, bs=2048 (2.39078) is lower than at lr=0.0002441, bs=512 (2.40141). — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[h100] (HIGH-LEVEL) At lr≈3.453e‑4, the best validation loss (2.38889) is achieved with bs=512. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)
[h200] (HIGH-LEVEL) Training at lr=0.0003453 with bs≈352 is near the optimal regime: increasing lr or bs beyond this point tends to increase validation loss, while moving further to lr≈0.0002441 or bs≫352 also hurts loss. — STATUS open (untested)
[h_new100] (HIGH-LEVEL) The loss valley appears when lr is at the lower end (1e‑4) *and* β is high (0.9). — STATUS open (untested)
[h_new101] (HIGH-LEVEL) Decreasing batch size from 2048 to 1024 leaves loss unchanged at lr=1e‑4, β=0.9. — STATUS open (untested)
[h_new102] (HIGH-LEVEL) Increasing batch size beyond 2048 does not lower loss; a plateau occurs. — STATUS open (untested)
[h_new104] (HIGH-LEVEL) The global optimum lies at lr=1e‑4, β=0.9, and bs≥2048, not merely among the tested cell parameters. — STATUS open (untested)
[h_new105] (HIGH-LEVEL) Learning‑rate schedules matter more than single‑epoch lr; the decay to 5e‑5 reduces loss. — STATUS open (untested)
[h_new110] (HIGH-LEVEL) The loss‑vs‑lr curve is monotone decreasing for lr ≤ 1e‑4; any increase above 1e‑4 is non‑optimal. — STATUS open (untested)
[h_new111] (HIGH-LEVEL) The optimal beta is not 0.9 but a narrow window around it (0.85‑0.95) where the loss is minimized. — STATUS open (untested)
[h50] (HIGH-LEVEL) The optimal learning rate is approximately 3.453e‑4, with a batch size of 512; further optimization yields diminishing returns. — STATUS open (untested)
[h12] (HIGH-LEVEL) Lower learning rates (~0.0003453) combined with very small batch sizes (~32‑352) give the lowest possible validation loss; higher rates or larger batches increase loss. — STATUS open (untested)
[h2] (narrow) Increasing batch size from 512 to 1024 at lr=0.0002441 does not lower loss below 2.40141. — STATUS open (inconclusive #1,#2,#7)
[h3] (narrow) At lr=0.0002441, loss is minimal at batch 512; batch 2048 yields higher loss 2.45412. — STATUS supported (supported by #2,#7; inconclusive #1,#3)
[h4] (narrow) Loss_0.0009766,2048 > Loss_0.0002441,2048. — STATUS open (inconclusive #1,#2,#3,#4,#7)

[h6] (narrow) Loss_1024 > Loss_512 (5.0 vs 2.40141). — STATUS supported (supported by #3,#7)

[h9] (narrow) Loss_0.0002441,2048 is highest among tested batch sizes at this LR. — STATUS open (inconclusive #3,#7)
[+hyp-2] (narrow) Learning rate at 2.441e-4 (with min‑batch 512) yields a loss lower than the loss at the intermediate hyper‑configuration (lr=7.812e-3, bs=736). — STATUS open (untested)
[hyp-135] (narrow) A larger batch size (736) together with the intermediate learning rate (7.812e-3) is more harmful to loss than a small batch (512) together with the low learning rate (2.441e-4). — STATUS open (untested)
[hh_new2] (narrow) At lr=0.0009766, loss_{2048}=2.39078 < loss_{512}=2.40141. — STATUS open (inconclusive #7)

[hh_new4] (narrow) Loss at lr=0.0009766, bs=2048 is lower than loss at same lr with any lower batch size. — STATUS open (untested)
[h101] (narrow, serves h100_a) At lr=3.453e‑4, test bs=352 does not lower loss. — STATUS supported (supported by #9; inconclusive #7)

[h202] (narrow) At lr=0.0003453, loss at bs=2048 is lower than at bs=1024. — STATUS supported (supported by #10)

[h_new103b] (narrow) At lr=1e‑4, reducing β from 0.9 to 0.5 does not increase loss relative to default 0.9. — STATUS open (untested)
[h_new106] (narrow) Gradient scaling (gradient clipping) has a negligible effect on loss at these hyper‑parameters. — STATUS open (untested)
[h_new107] (narrow) Weight‑decay=0.1 and 1e‑4 are equally effective; using 0.5 yields no loss change. — STATUS open (untested)
[h_new108] (narrow) Batch normalization does not change loss at these settings; disabling it leaves loss unchanged. — STATUS open (untested)

[h100_b] (narrow, serves h100) At lr=3.453e‑4, increasing batch size to 1024 may lower loss relative to 512. — STATUS open (untested)
